channel.title: Obsidian Vault
channel.generator: Webpage HTML Export plugin for Obsidian

item.title: Test 1 - Continuous Tests Focus Points 2026 1
item.description:
You will notice some focal points have a yellow highlight. These tend to be areas chosen for short answer/longer answer questions that are not multichoice. Demonstrate the use of familiar prefixes and suffixes e.g. Hyper-, -emia etc Prefixes Suffixes Key Terms (from the glossary) Anatomy — the study of the structure of the body and the relationships between body parts. For example, identifying where the heart sits in the thoracic cavity. Physiology — the study of the functions of body parts and how they work. For example, understanding how the heart pumps blood. Anatomy and physiology are always studied together because structure determines function. Respiration — the process by which cells produce energy (ATP) from oxygen and glucose, releasing carbon dioxide as a waste product. Note: this refers to cellular respiration, not just breathing (which is called ventilation). Atom — the smallest unit of a chemical element that retains the properties of that element. Atoms make up molecules, which make up the chemical level of body organisation — the very foundation of the levels of organisation you studied. Sebaceous — relating to sebum, an oily/waxy substance. Sebaceous glands are found in the skin and secrete sebum into hair follicles to lubricate and waterproof the skin and hair (part of the integumentary system).  Describe what determines if something is living or non-livingCharacteristics of All Living Organisms> A common mnemonic to remember these is MRSGREN — Movement, Respiration, Sensitivity, Growth, Reproduction, Excretion, Nutrition. 1. Movement Living things move — either the whole organism (e.g. walking, swimming) or internally (e.g. blood moving through vessels, gut contracting). Even plants move slowly toward light. 2. Respiration Living things produce energy (ATP) from nutrients at the cellular level. This is cellular respiration, not just breathing. Every living cell carries out some form of respiration to fuel its activities. 3. Sensitivity / Irritability Living things detect and respond to changes in their environment (stimuli). For example, pulling your hand away from heat, pupils dilating in dim light, or a plant turning toward sunlight. 4. Growth Living things increase in size or complexity over time by producing more cells or increasing cell size. Growth is organised and controlled — not just random accumulation of material. 5. Reproduction Living things produce offspring, either sexually or asexually. This is essential for the continuation of a species. Note — an individual organism can survive without reproducing, but life as a whole cannot. 6. Excretion Living things remove the waste products of their own metabolic processes. For example, the lungs excrete CO₂, the kidneys excrete urea. This is different from simply eliminating undigested food. 7. Nutrition Living things take in and use nutrients to fuel metabolism, growth, and repair. This could be through eating, photosynthesis, or absorbing nutrients.  Understand and demonstrate the use of directional terms (e.g. Anterior, lateral)Directional Terms Definitions Superior (cranial): toward the head or upper part of a structure Inferior (caudal): away from the head end or toward lower part of a structure Ventral (anterior): toward or at front of the body Dorsal (posterior): toward or at the back of the body Medial: toward or at the midline of the body Lateral: away from the midline of the body Intermediate: between a more medial and a more lateral structure Proximal: closer to the point of attachment of a limb to the body trunk Distal: farther from the point of attachment of a limb to the body trunk Superficial (external): toward or at the body surface Deep (internal): away from the body surface (more internal) Directional Terms (Diagram)
 Explain the major cavities of the human body and their subdivisions, including being able to identify the location of various organs within these (e.g. Spinal cavity, thoracic cavity)Major Body Cavities  Cranial cavity in skull  Vertebral/spinal cavity within vertebral column  Thoracic cavity surrounded by ribs  Abdominopelvic cavity – more inferior
Major Body Cavities and their subdivisions (Diagram)  Describe the levels of organisation of the human body including examples (e.g. Tissues, organs etc)Levels of Organisation Chemical level – Simplest level Atoms combine to form molecules, molecules form organelles Cellular level Cells – smallest living units Tissue level Tissues – groups of similar cells Four basic tissue types Organ level Organ – made up of at least 2 tissue types Complex functions Organ system Organs work together for common purpose Organismal level - Highest level Result of all structural levels working together  Name and describe the function of structures found in a typical cell (e.g. Mitochondria)
Diagram Key & Explanation
Membrane (Boundary structures)
Nucleus (Control & genetic)



Organelle (Specialised structures)







Cytoplasm (Fluid & support)
 Describe cell specialisation (e.g. Why are specific cells the shape they are, to do the job they do: RBC, nerve cell, melanocyte, Merkel cell, phagocyte)
Cell Specialisation  Briefly explain passive transport and active transport processes (e.g. Diffusion, facilitated diffusion, osmosis etc. In context)
Overview of passive and active transport processes

Passive Transport




Active Transport





Solutions & Tonicity


In Context (Nursing Relevance)

 Differentiate between labile, stable and permanent cells and give examples
Labile Cells
Stable Cells
Permanent Cells  Describe the differences between somatic and reproductive cell division and the functions of each (including the stages of each process)
Mitosis and Meiosis (somatic and reproductive cell division)
Comparison & Features between Mitosis and Meiosis  Describe the stages of mitosisPMATC The key mnemonic to lock in is PMATC — Prophase, Metaphase, Anaphase, Telophase, Cytokinesis. Interphase comes before all of these but is not technically a stage of mitosis itself — it is the preparation phase.
Mitosis Stages (Diagram)





TISSUES SUMMARY TABLE  Describe the major functions of the 4 types of tissuesFunctions of the 4 types of tissues What are the functions of epithelial tissue? What are the functions of connective tissue? What are the functional characteristics of all muscle tissue? What are the functional characteristics of nervous tissue  Identify various tissues by looking at their structure
Tissue Structure Diagrams


 Explain how the structure of specific tissue helps the tissue with its function (e.g. How does the dense irregular connective tissue of the dermis help the dermis to do its job?)Structure of specific tissues and how it helps with function The rule: Every feature of a tissue exists because of the job it does. 1. Dermis — dense irregular connective tissue Collagen fibres run in all directions within an extracellular matrix. Fibroblasts produce and maintain these fibres. Because fibres go every way, skin resists tearing from any direction. 2. Alveoli — simple squamous epithelium One layer of flat cells with flattened nuclei, minimal cytoplasm, on a basement membrane. Wall is as thin as possible so O₂ and CO₂ diffuse across quickly. 3. Skin/mouth/oesophagus — stratified squamous epithelium Many layers of flat cells. Outer cells are dead and packed with keratin. New cells made at the basement membrane push upward to replace worn-away ones. Barrier never breaks down. 4. Heart — cardiac muscle Striated cells (organised actin and myosin = forceful contraction). One nucleus per cell. Connected by intercalated discs — gap junctions pass electrical signals instantly, desmosomes hold cells together. Whole heart contracts as one unit. 5. Airways — pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium Tall cells (need space for cilia machinery) all sitting on a basement membrane. Goblet cells produce mucus to trap pathogens. Cilia sweep mucus upward and out of the lungs. 6. Gut/bladder/blood vessels — smooth muscle Spindle-shaped, one nucleus, no striations. Contracts slowly, never fatigues, can shorten greatly. Perfect for hollow organs that need to move or change size automatically for hours.  Describe special characteristics of epithelial tissue (e.g. Avascular)Special characteristics of epithelial tissue Avascular — no blood vessels. Gets nutrients by diffusion from tissue underneath. Basement membrane — anchors the epithelium to the tissue below. Tightly packed cells — no gaps, forms a continuous sheet. Polarity — one side faces outward (apical), one side faces downward (basal). Innervated — has nerve supply. Regenerates fast — constantly replacing worn-out cells. Forms boundaries — always sits between inside and outside of the body.  Describe how epithelial tissue is classified by layers and shapeEpithelial tissue classification by layers and shape Layers: Simple = one layer Stratified = many layers Pseudostratified = looks layered but is actually one layer (all cells touch the basement membrane) Shape: Squamous = flat Cuboidal = cube Columnar = tall Transitional = changes shape when stretched Example: simple squamous = one layer of flat cells. Stratified squamous = many layers of flat cells.  Describe the functions and locations of specific epithelial tissue with (e.g. Stratified epithelium)Functions and locations of specific epithelial tissue (Table)  Describe the function and locations of connective tissues (e.g. Adipose, hyaline cartilage)How to Identify Connective Tissue An extracellular matrix — non-living material surrounding the cells Fibres in that matrix (collagen, elastic, or reticular) Scattered cells — not tightly packed Found internally — never on a body surface Variable vascularity — some types highly vascular, some none at all Function and locations of connective tissues Areolar (loose CT)  Where: under skin, around organs and blood vessels. What it does: fills gaps between structures and holds organs in place. Adipose (fat)  Where: under skin, around kidneys, heart, in breasts and abdomen. What it does: stores energy, insulates against heat loss, cushions organs. Reticular  Where: liver, spleen, lymph nodes, bone marrow. What it does: forms a mesh framework that supports soft organs — like scaffolding. Dense regular CT  Where: tendons and ligaments. What it does: very strong in one direction. Parallel fibres act like a rope — perfect for connecting muscle to bone or bone to bone. Dense irregular CT  Where: dermis of skin, joint capsules. What it does: resists tearing from any direction because fibres run every which way. Hyaline cartilage  Where: joint surfaces, trachea rings, ribs, embryonic skeleton. What it does: smooth surface reduces joint friction. Slightly flexible so it absorbs compression. Avascular so heals slowly. Fibrocartilage  Where: intervertebral discs, knee menisci, pubic symphysis. What it does: toughest cartilage. Acts as a shock absorber between bones. Elastic cartilage  Where: outer ear, epiglottis. What it does: holds shape but can bend and spring back. More flexible than hyaline. Bone  Where: skeleton. What it does: supports the body, protects organs, stores minerals, produces blood cells in bone marrow. Cells: osteoblasts build it, osteocytes maintain it, osteoclasts break it down. Blood  Where: blood vessels everywhere. What it does: transports oxygen, nutrients, hormones, waste and immune cells around the body. Note: it is connective tissue because it has cells floating in a liquid matrix called plasma.  Identify tissues as connective based on their characteristics (e.g. Connective tissue has fibres)Identify tissues as connective based on their characteristics If you can tick these boxes, it is connective tissue. Has an extracellular matrix The defining feature. There is non-living material surrounding the cells — this can be liquid (blood), gel-like (cartilage) or solid (bone). Contains fibres Most connective tissues contain one or more of these fibres sitting in the matrix: Collagen fibres — strong, resist stretching Elastic fibres — can stretch and spring back Reticular fibres — fine mesh-like network Cells are scattered Cells sit spread out within the matrix. They are NOT tightly packed together like epithelial cells. Always found internally Connective tissue is never exposed to the outside world. It always sits inside the body. Comes from mesenchyme All connective tissue originates from the same embryonic tissue called mesenchyme. Variable vascularity Some types are richly supplied with blood vessels (bone, areolar). Some have none at all (cartilage, which is why it heals so slowly).  Describe the different membranes found within the human body (e.g. Serous membranes)Different membranes within the body Serous membrane Where: lines closed body cavities — pleura (lungs), pericardium (heart), peritoneum (abdomen). What it does: produces watery fluid so organs can slide against each other without friction. Mucous membrane Where: lines cavities open to the outside — airways, gut, urinary tract, reproductive tract. What it does: produces mucus to trap pathogens and particles. Also helps with absorption and secretion. Cutaneous membrane Where: entire outer surface of the body — this is just the skin. What it does: tough waterproof barrier against damage, pathogens and water loss. The only dry membrane. Synovial membrane Where: inside moveable joints — knee, hip, shoulder. What it does: produces synovial fluid to lubricate joints and deliver nutrients to cartilage inside.  Describe the functions and locations of the different muscle tissues in the body (e.g. Cardiac, skeletal and smooth)Functions and locations of different muscle tissues in the body Muscle Tissue — All muscle can: Receive signals Contract Stretch Spring back Where: Attached to bones and skin Function: Moves the body, keeps posture, produces heat Features: Striated (striped) Voluntary (you control it) Many nuclei per cell Notes: Gets tired (fatigues) Wastes away quickly with inactivity Where: Heart wall only Function: Pumps blood nonstop Features: Striated Involuntary (you don’t control it) One nucleus per cell Has intercalated discs (helps the heart contract as one unit) Notes: Cannot regenerate — heart attack damage is permanent Where: Walls of hollow organs (gut, bladder, blood vessels, uterus, airways) Function: Moves substances automatically through organs Features: Not striated Involuntary One nucleus per cell Spindle-shaped Notes: Contracts slowly, never fatigues Can shorten much more than skeletal muscle Skeletal = you control it Cardiac = heart only, never stops Smooth = automatic, never tires  Describe the structure and function of neurons and neurogliaStructure and function of neurons and neuroglia Neurons are specialized cells designed to transmit information. They have three main parts: Dendrites Short, branching extensions from the cell body Function: receive incoming signals from other neurons or receptors Cell body (Soma) Contains the nucleus and most organelles Function: integrates incoming signals and maintains cell functions Axon A long projection that carries signals away from the cell body Can be very long (e.g., from spinal cord to muscles) Ends in axon terminals, which release neurotransmitters to communicate with other cells Myelin sheath A fatty insulating layer around some axons Produced by certain neuroglia Function: increases the speed of nerve impulse transmission Nodes of Ranvier Gaps between myelin segments Allow faster signal conduction by letting impulses "jump" between nodes Neurons are responsible for communication within the nervous system through electrical and chemical signals. Steps of signal transmission: Dendrites receive a stimulus The cell body processes the information An electrical impulse travels along the axon At the axon terminal, neurotransmitters are released across a synapse to another cell Types of neurons (briefly): Sensory neurons: carry information from receptors to the central nervous system Motor neurons: carry signals from the central nervous system to muscles or glands Interneurons: connect neurons within the brain and spinal cord Neuroglia are non-excitable cells that support, protect, and maintain neurons. They are more numerous than neurons and are essential for proper nervous system function. Astrocytes Provide structural support Help regulate the chemical environment Contribute to the blood-brain barrier Oligodendrocytes (central nervous system) Form the myelin sheath around axons Schwann cells (peripheral nervous system) Also form myelin sheath Assist in nerve repair Microglia Act as immune cells Remove waste, pathogens, and damaged cells Ependymal cells Line cavities in the brain and spinal cord Help produce and circulate cerebrospinal fluid Support and protect neurons Supply nutrients and oxygen Form myelin sheath for insulation Maintain homeostasis (stable internal environment) Defend against infection and remove debris Neurons are specialized cells that transmit electrical signals for communication. Neuroglia are supporting cells that protect, nourish, and assist neurons in functioning efficiently.  Describe the stages of tissue repair (e.g. Inflammation, granulation etc.)Stages of tissue repair Tissue repair is the process the body uses to fix damaged tissue. It can result in: Regeneration – replacement with the same type of tissue (normal function restored) Fibrosis – replacement with scar tissue (does not function the same) Occurs immediately after injury Blood vessels constrict to reduce bleeding A blood clot forms Stops blood loss Forms a temporary barrier to protect the wound The body responds to injury Blood vessels dilate (become wider) White blood cells move to the area Redness Heat Swelling Pain Prevents infection Removes dead cells and pathogens Prepares the area for healing Granulation tissue forms (new soft tissue) New capillaries (blood vessels) grow Fibroblasts produce collagen (connective tissue fibres) Restores blood supply Begins rebuilding the tissue structure Tissue is replaced with same type of cells Normal function returns Tissue is replaced with scar tissue (fibrous connective tissue) Function is reduced or lost Completes the healing process Provides permanent repair Haemostasis – stops bleeding Inflammation – cleans and protects Organisation (granulation) – rebuilds tissue and blood supply Regeneration/Fibrosis – final repair (normal tissue or scar)  Explain the roles and uses of micro-organisms (e.g. Making antibiotics)Roles and Uses of Micro-organisms Micro-organisms are tiny living things that are found everywhere: in soil, water, air, and on and inside our bodies. Why they are important: They break down dead plants and animals, recycling nutrients back into the environment. Some bacteria help plants grow by fixing nitrogen in soil. Many live on our bodies as normal flora, protecting us from harmful microbes. Humans use micro-organisms to make foods (bread, cheese, yoghurt, beer, wine). Some micro-organisms are used to make medicines, especially antibiotics, such as penicillin made by the fungus Penicillium.  Describe how bacteria are classified based on staining, arrangement and shapesBacteria classification by shapes and arrangement Bacteria are grouped so we can identify them more easily. Gram-positive bacteria stain purple (thick cell wall). Gram-negative bacteria stain pink (thin cell wall). This matters because some antibiotics work better on one group than the other. Cocci – round (spheres) Bacilli – rod-shaped Spiral bacteria – twisted or spiral-shaped Diplococci – pairs Streptococci – chains Staphylococci – clusters (grape-like)  Explain the structural features of bacteria (e.g. Slime layer)Structural Features of Bacteria Bacteria are simple cells but have important parts: Cell wall – gives shape and protection Plasma membrane – controls what enters and leaves the cell Capsule / slime layer – sticky outer layer that helps bacteria attach to surfaces and avoid the immune system Flagella – tail-like parts for movement Pili/fimbriae – help bacteria attach or exchange DNA Ribosomes – make proteins DNA (chromosome & plasmids) – genetic instructions  Explain how and why some bacteria form endosporesEndospores Some bacteria protect themselves by forming endospores. Endospores form when conditions are harsh (heat, lack of food, chemicals). The bacterium dehydrates, seals its DNA in a tough coat, and becomes inactive. Endospores are very resistant and can survive for years. When conditions improve, the spore becomes an active bacterium again. Example: Clostridium species.  Name some bacteria and the diseases they causeBacteria and the Diseases They Cause Examples include: Staphylococcus – skin infections, boils, wound infections Streptococcus – sore throat, pneumonia, rheumatic fever Escherichia coli (E. coli) – urinary tract infections, diarrhoea Clostridium – tetanus, botulism, gangrene Haemophilus influenzae – meningitis, ear infections  Describe how bacteria grow and reproduceHow bacteria grow and reproduce Bacteria reproduce by binary fission. One cell divides into two identical cells. Under ideal conditions, this can happen every 20–30 minutes. Growth depends on: Temperature Moisture pH Oxygen Some bacteria need oxygen (aerobes), some don’t (anaerobes).  Describe how viruses are classified and characteristics of viruses (e.g. Ability to mutate)Viruses: Classification and Characteristics Viruses are not living cells. Key characteristics: Much smaller than bacteria Contain DNA or RNA, not both Surrounded by a protein coat (capsid) Some have an outer envelope Must infect a host cell to reproduce They mutate easily, especially RNA viruses Viruses are classified by: Type of genetic material Shape How they replicate  Name some viruses and the diseases they causeViruses and Diseases They Cause Examples: Influenza – flu Measles virus – measles Mumps virus – mumps HIV – AIDS Herpes simplex – cold sores Varicella-zoster – chickenpox Coronavirus – COVID-19  Briefly explain how viruses replicateHow Viruses Replicate Virus attaches to a host cell Virus enters the cell Viral DNA/RNA is released Host cell makes new viral parts New viruses are assembled New viruses leave the cell (often killing it)  Explain what is meant by the term “opportunistic pathogens” and “opportunistic infections”Opportunistic Pathogens and Infections An opportunistic pathogen normally does not cause disease. It causes problems when the body’s defences are weakened. This happens when: Immunity is low Antibiotics destroy normal flora Bacteria enter an unusual site Example: Candida albicans normally lives on skin but causes thrush if immunity is reduced.  Explain basic characteristics and structural features of fungi: yeasts and mouldsFungi: Yeasts and Moulds Fungi are eukaryotic organisms (like human cells). Single-celled Reproduce by budding Example: Candida albicans Multicellular Made of hyphae forming a mycelium Produce spores Cell walls contain chitin  Provide examples of fungal infections and the organisms that cause these infectionsFungal infections and the organisms that cause these Examples: Infection: athlete’s foot, ringworm Cause: Dermatophytes Infection: thrush Cause: Candida albicans Infection: pneumonia (immunocompromised) Cause:Pneumocystis jirovecii Infection: skin rash (tinea versicolor) Cause: - Malassezia furfur  Explain some issues related to the presence of fungi (e.g. Spores)Issues related to Fungi Fungal spores are airborne Can cause: Allergies Respiratory problems Skin irritation Some fungi produce toxins that can damage organs or cause cancer  Explain basic characteristics and structural features of protozoa (e.g. Cilia)Protozoa: Characteristics Protozoa are single-celled eukaryotes. They are classified by how they move: Flagella – Giardia Cilia – Balantidium Pseudopodia – Amoeba Non-motile – Plasmodium  Provide examples of protozoal infectionsProtozoal Infections Examples: Giardia lamblia – diarrhoea Entamoeba histolytica – amoebic dysentery Plasmodium – malaria (spread by mosquitoes)  Explain basic characteristics and structural features of helminths and ectoparasitesHelminths and Ectoparasites Multicellular worms Examples: roundworms, tapeworms Eggs passed in faeces Live on the outside of the body Examples: lice, mites, fleas, ticks Can transmit other diseases  Explain an issue relating to ectoparasites (e.g. Continuous irritation)Issue with Ectoparasites Constant itching and irritation Skin damage Secondary infections Disease transmission (e.g. typhus)  Explain what is meant by the term “normal flora” and give examplesNormal Flora Micro-organisms that normally live on/in the body Help protect by: Competing with pathogens Producing useful substances Example: E. coli in the gut  Explain the difference between normal flora and transient floraNormal vs Transient Flora Normal flora: permanent Transient flora: temporary (picked up from environment)  Describe what sterile means and where the sterile areas of the body areSterile meaning and areas found Sterile means no micro-organisms present. Sterile areas include: Blood Internal organs Foetus Serous cavities  Explain the difference between infection and diseaseInfection vs Disease Infection: micro-organisms enter and multiply Disease: infection causes damage and symptoms  Explain different types of infections (e.g. Subclinical, endogenous etc.)Infection Types Subclinical – no symptoms Endogenous – from normal flora Exogenous – from outside source Localised – one area Systemic – whole body Opportunistic – when immunity is low  Explain factors that influence patient susceptibility (e.g. Reduced immunity)Factors Affecting Susceptibility Weak immune system Age (very young or old) Poor nutrition Stress Chronic disease Poor hygiene  Briefly explain the disease process and the development of disease (e.g. Prodromal period, convalescing etc.)Development of disease Stages: Incubation – pathogen multiplies Prodromal – vague symptoms Acute phase – worst symptoms Decline – symptoms reduce Convalescence – recovery  Explain common signs and symptoms of disease (e.g. Fever, rash etc.)Common signs and symptoms of disease Fever Inflammation Pain Rash Fatigue Abnormal test results  Explain the difference between communicable, non-communicable and contagious diseases and be able to give examplesCommunicable vs Non-Communicable and contagious diseases Communicable – spread person to person (flu) Contagious – very easily spread (measles) Non-communicable – not spread person to person (diabetes)  Describe the various reservoirs of infectionReservoirs of Infection Humans Animals Soil, water, objects (fomites)  Describe the portals of entry and exitPortals of Entry and Exit Entry: Skin Lungs Gut Urogenital tract Blood Exit: Coughing/sneezing Faeces Urine Blood Skin lesions  Explain how micro-organisms are transmittedWays of transmission of Micro-organisms Direct contact Indirect contact (objects) Droplets Airborne Food/water Insects (vectors)  Explain what is meant by the term “epidemiology”Epidemiology definition Epidemiology is the study of how diseases spread, who they affect, and how to control them.  Explain what a notifiable disease is and provide some examples of notifiable diseases in New ZealandNotifiable Diseases Meaning Tuberculosis (TB) Measles Hepatitis AIDS Pertussis  Explain what a nosocomial/HCAI infection is and common causative agentsNosocomial / HCAI Infections & Their Common Causative Agents A nosocomial or healthcare‑associated infection (HCAI) is an infection acquired during healthcare treatment that was not present when the patient was admitted. These infections commonly occur due to invasive procedures, contaminated equipment, or poor hand hygiene in healthcare settings. Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA) causes wound, skin, and bloodstream infections. Escherichia coli commonly causes urinary tract infections, especially in catheterised patients. Clostridioides difficile causes diarrhoea, particularly after antibiotic use. Pseudomonas and Klebsiella species cause pneumonia and bloodstream infections in vulnerable patients.  Explain factors that predispose patients to HCAIPatient's Predisposing Factors to HCAI Surgery Catheters Poor immunity Lengthy hospital stay  Explain which body sites are affected by HCAIBody sites affected by HCAI Urinary tract Wounds Respiratory tract Bloodstream  Explain factors that contribute to the occurrence of HCAIFactors That Contribute to the Occurrence of HCAI Poor hand hygiene allows micro‑organisms to spread between patients. Invasive procedures such as surgery, catheters, and IV lines create entry points for pathogens. Contaminated equipment or surfaces increase the risk of infection. Overcrowding and vulnerable patients with weakened immunity increase transmission risk. Inappropriate antibiotic use encourages the development of resistant organisms.  List the components of the chain of infection and explain how the chain of infection can be broken at different linksChain of Infection & How to break it Germ (Infectious Agent) → The thing that causes disease Where it lives (Reservoir) → Where the germ stays (people, animals, surfaces) Way out (Portal of Exit) → How it leaves (coughing, blood, fluids) How it spreads (Transmission) → How it travels (touch, air, droplets) Way in (Portal of Entry) → How it enters the body (mouth, nose, cuts) Person at risk (Susceptible Host) → Someone who can get sick Kill the germ → Clean, disinfect, use medicine Clean the area → Keep surroundings safe Block the exit → Cover coughs, wear masks Stop the spread → Wash hands, use PPE Block entry → Protect wounds, use gloves
Protect people → Vaccines, healthy lifestyle  Explain the difference between sterilisation and disinfectionSterilisation vs Disinfection Sterilisation: kills all microbes incl. spores (e.g. autoclave) Disinfection: reduces microbes on objects, not spores  Explain standard precautions and additional precautionsStandard and Additional Precautions in Healthcare Settings Hand hygiene is performed before and after patient contact to prevent the spread of micro‑organisms. Gloves and gowns are worn to protect both the patient and healthcare worker from contact with body fluids. Safe waste handling is used to prevent exposure to infectious materials and contamination of the environment. Contact precautions are used when infections spread through direct contact or contaminated surfaces, such as MRSA. Droplet precautions are used for infections spread by coughing or sneezing, such as influenza. Airborne precautions are used for infections spread through the air over long distances, such as tuberculosis or measles.  Explain what antiseptics areAntiseptics explained Antiseptics are chemicals used on living tissue such as skin or wounds to reduce or kill micro‑organisms. They help prevent infection and sepsis during wound care and medical procedures. Antiseptics reduce microbial numbers but do not completely sterilise tissue. They differ from disinfectants, which are used on non‑living surfaces.  Explain how antibacterial drugs kill bacteriaHow Antibacterial Drugs Work Antibacterial Drugs will: Damages the cell walls of bacteria Stop protein production since bacteria needs proteins to grow Disrupt metabolism which can starve bacteria Damages membranes DNA or contents, which prevent proliferation and survival  Explain what is meant by antibiotic resistanceAntibiotic Resistance Explanation Bacteria survive antibiotics Caused by misuse or incomplete courses of antibiotics Makes infections harder to treat
